title: Deep Ocean Characteristics and Exploration
focus: Physical conditions, biodiversity in the Mesopelagic Zone, and the significance of deep ocean exploration.
rationale: Covers fundamental oceanography concepts and resource potential, highly relevant for UPSC prelims.
sectionExcerpt: Deep ocean conditions include an average temperature of 4 degrees Celsius and extreme pressure. The Mesopelagic Zone (200m-1000m) contains about 90 percent of the world's fish by weight. Exploration is significant for energy sources, new drugs, polymetallic nodules, and climate change mitigation.
evidenceBullets: Average temperature is 4 degrees Celsius with pressure 40 to 110 times Earth's atmosphere.
Mesopelagic Zone (200m-1000m) holds about 90 percent of world's fish by weight.
Exploration yields energy sources like methane hydrate, polymetallic nodules, and new drugs.

title: Polar Anticyclone
focus: Formation mechanism and air circulation patterns of polar anticyclones.
rationale: Core climatology concept detailing high-pressure system formation and wind direction.
sectionExcerpt: Polar anticyclones are high-pressure wind systems created by intense cooling of surface air layers. Surface cooling causes dense air to sink, which is replaced by high-altitude air, building high pressure. Winds flow outward from the center, circulating clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and anti-clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere.
evidenceBullets: Created by intense cooling of surface air causing dense air to sink.
High-pressure area surrounded by low-pressure zones with outward flowing winds.
Circulation is clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and anti-clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere.

title: Thirstwave
focus: Definition, criteria, and factors driving extreme atmospheric thirst.
rationale: Emerging climate terminology important for environment and geography sections.
sectionExcerpt: A thirstwave involves periods of extreme atmospheric thirst that rapidly dry out the landscape, defined by high dryness and aggressive moisture demand. It occurs when daily evaporative demand exceeds its historical 90th percentile for at least 3 consecutive days. Factors driving this include temperature, wind speed, humidity, and sunshine.
evidenceBullets: Defined by high dryness and aggressive atmospheric demand for moisture.
Requires at least 3 consecutive days of evaporative demand greater than the historical 90th percentile.
Driven by temperature, wind speed, humidity, and sunshine.

title: Mercator's Map Projection
focus: Characteristics, distortions, and recent campaigns against the Mercator projection.
rationale: Important cartography concept with contemporary relevance due to the African Union's campaign.
sectionExcerpt: Designed in 1569 by Gerardus Mercator, this conformal map preserves local shapes and angles but distorts scale, making polar landmasses appear larger and equatorial regions smaller. For example, Africa appears roughly the size of Greenland despite being 14 times larger. The African Union endorsed the Correct the Map campaign to replace it with alternatives like the Equal Earth map.
evidenceBullets: Conformal map designed in 1569 that preserves local shapes and angles.
Distorts scale by enlarging landmasses near the poles and shrinking equatorial regions.
African Union endorsed the Correct the Map campaign to replace it with the Equal Earth map.

title: Arabian Sea Mini Warm Pool
focus: Definition, location, duration, and impact on the Indian Summer Monsoon.
rationale: Crucial Indian monsoon mechanism and regional climate phenomenon.
sectionExcerpt: The Arabian Sea Mini Warm Pool (MWP) is a patch of unusually warm sea surface temperatures (exceeding 28.5 degrees Celsius) in the southeastern Arabian Sea near the Kerala coast. It forms annually during April and May before the Indian Summer Monsoon and acts as a self-correcting mechanism to help the monsoon recover from previous El Nino disruptions.
evidenceBullets: Located in the southeastern Arabian Sea near the Kerala coast with SST exceeding 28.5 degrees Celsius.
Forms annually during April and May just before the Indian Summer Monsoon.
Acts as a self-correcting mechanism helping the monsoon recover from previous El Nino disruptions.

title: National Centre of Polar and Ocean Research (NCPOR)
focus: Genesis, governing body, key roles, and newly launched facilities of NCPOR.
rationale: Key Indian scientific institution involved in polar research and the Deep Ocean Mission.
sectionExcerpt: NCPOR is an autonomous institution under the Ministry of Earth Sciences, established in 1998. It maintains India's research stations in Antarctica (Maitri, Bharati), the Arctic (Himadri), and the Himalayas (Himansh). It leads the Deep Ocean Mission and aids in implementing India's Arctic Policy and Antarctica Act. Recent facilities include Polar Bhavan and Sagar Bhavan.
evidenceBullets: Autonomous institution of the Ministry of Earth Sciences established in 1998.
Maintains research stations: Maitri and Bharati (Antarctica), Himadri (Arctic), and Himansh (Himalayas).
Leads the Deep Ocean Mission and implements India's Arctic Policy and Antarctica Act.
Recently launched Polar Bhavan (laboratories/museum) and Sagar Bhavan (ice core laboratories).
